columns.text:
An icon of the late war, the Panzerfaust was the world's first practical disposable anti-tank weapon. Its simple arrangement of a shaped-charge warhead mounted atop a lightweight launch tube proved so successful that the same basic concept has been adopted by militaries around the world, and remains in service over eighty years later.

Developed by Hugo Schneider AG, the Panzerfaust was designed around one goal: putting an effective anti-tank weapon into the hands of as many soldiers as possible. Its exceptionally simple and economical construction allowed it to be produced in astronomical numbers, with millions issued to German troops during the Second World War. The "Tank Fist," as it became known, excelled in close-quarters combat, where its portability allowed infantry to move quickly between positions before striking enemy armor with a HEAT warhead capable of penetrating over 200 mm of steel. Against the tanks of its day, few could hope to withstand a direct hit.

In ARMA III, the Spearhead 1944 DLC features both the Panzerfaust 30 (Groß) and Panzerfaust 60 variants, each named after its effective combat range in meters.

• Launchers are thin, sand-coloured tubes featuring a large shaped charge on the muzzle.
• Units carrying them use a unique under-the-shoulder animation.

Equip the Panzerfaust 30 or Panzerfaust 60 under the Launcher Slot from the Spearhead DLC.
Make sure it is loaded by checking to see if the tube has the grenade on the end, if not then the tubed is used, and cannot be reloaded. If you are unable to fire, make sure the tube has the Warhead Panzerfaust round within the ammo slot. If it is not, but the tube is unfired, it may appear as if the tube is ready to fire, but without the round, it is unable to do so.

Use the sights to line up the target. The Panzerfaust 30 has a maximum engagement range of 30 meters, and as such only has one zero setting. Conversely, the Panzerfaust 60 is able to engage up to 80 meters, by using the ladder sight, you can switch between differing zeros. It's recommended that you aim center mass, as the innate inaccuracies of the round, and its high-arc may make precise firing difficult. Large targets such as the engine block are viable within 30 meters, however, any further than that one may struggle. Further, engaging moving targets at a distance will be difficult, as the round suffers from a very slow velocity.

blocks.text:
When engaging armored targets, such as the M4 Sherman, recommended engagement ranges are within 30 meters. From outside that distance, both Panzerfausts struggle to penetrate for immediate kills, however, large damage may still be inflicted upon the target and its occupants. When in doubt, wait for the closest possible engagement range.
Once firing is complete, dispose of the weapon, as spent tubes are useless and unable to be reloaded.
